content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

June 30, 1977

Clayton P. Roche
Deputy Attorney General
Department of Justice
State Building
San Francisco, CA 94102

Re: Opinion No. CV 77/82

Dear Mr. Roche:

This letter is in response to your request for our
views on Attorney General Opinion Request CV 77/82. Initially,
to the extent that your response interprets provisions of the
Political Reform Act, we request that your opinion be contained
in an indexed letter rather than a published opinion.

The facts as we understand them are as follows:
A California Highway Patrol employee purchased a part interest
in an Arco gas station in Yreka. The particular station in ques-
tion has the lowest gasoline prices in the city and Arco is one
of several companies which is authorized to do business with the
State of California. In light of these facts you have asked two
questions: First, can the California Highway Patrol prohibit an
employee from purchasing supplies and services for the State of
California from a business entity in which the employee is a part
owner? Second, can the California Highway Patrol prohibit other
employees from purchasing supplies and services for the State of
California from a business entity in which another Department em-
ployee is a part owner?

At the outset, we wish to emphasize that our comments
are limited to an interpretation of the provisions of the Politi-
cal Reform Act. We make no comments with respect to the applica-
bility of Government Code Sections 1090 et seq. or the California
Highway Patrol's incompatibility statement.

The Act provides in Government Code Section 87100 1/ that

No public official at any level of state or
local government shall make, participate in
making or in any way attempt to use his of-
ficial position to influence a governmental

1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6441

Clayton P. Roche
Page two
June 30, 1977


decision in which he knows or has reason to know
he has a financial interest.

The term "financial interest" is defined in Section 87103:

An official has a financial interest in a de-
cision within the meaning of Section 87100
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the de-
cision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(b) Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest
worth more than one thousand dollars ($1,000);

(c) Any source of income, other than loans
by a commercial lending institution in the
regular course of business, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by or promised to the public official
within twelve months prior to the time when
the decision is made; or

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management....

Thus, assuming that the employee in question has a
financial interest by virtue of both his investment in the sta-
tion and the income which he receives from it, the determina-
tive question which must be resolved is whether the employee
is making or participating in any governmental decisions which
may foreseeably and materially affect this financial interest.

The Commission has provided by Regulation 18700(b)(4)
that

A public official "makes a governmental de-
cision," "...when he or she, acting within the
authority of his or her office:...

(4) Enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency;....

Thus, the employee makes a decision pursuant to this regulation

Clayton P. Roche
Page three
June 30, 1977


whenever he purchases gasoline for his state car since, at the
time of purchase, he has entered into a contract on the state's
behalf. A fortiori, this decision will have a foreseeable ef-
fect on the Arco station from which the purchase is made. More-
over, we think the effect of such a decision also will be material
pursuant to Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702. Sub-
section (b) of this regulation provides several quantitative
guidelines which may be appropriate in some instances. However,
the general rule is set forth in subsection (a) which provides:

     (a) The financial effect of a governmental de-
cision on a financial interest of a public of-
ficial is material if, at the time the official
makes, participates in making or attempts to use
his or her official position to influence the
making of the decision, in light of all the circum-
stances and facts known at the time of the decis-
ion, the official knows or has reason to know
that the existence of the financial interest
might interfere with the official's performance
of his or her duties in an impartial manner free
from bias.

                                      Emphasis added.

In the instant case, we think the effect of the decision is material
since it is obvious that the employee may be biased in determin-
ing whether to purchase gasoline from his own service station. Un-
der those facts, we think it is unnecessary to apply the quantita-
tive standards set forth in subsection (b) of the regulation. Ac-
cordingly, we conclude that the employee generally would be pro-
hibited from making or participating in a decision to purchase gaso-
line at the Arco station in which he has a financial interest.

     There is, however, one hypothetical set of circumstances
which would permit the owner to purchase gasoline for a state car
from his station. If a Highway Patrol directive or a senior of-
ficer, who did not have a financial interest in the decision,
ordered all employees to purchase gasoline from a specific sta-
tion or oil company, or from the station which they knew to have
the lowest price, the owner would be permitted to purchase gaso-
line from his station if such a purchase complied with the pro-
visions of the directive. Obviously, the officer with the owner-
ship interest would be prohibited from making, participating in
or using his official position to influence such a decision by
the Highway Patrol or one of its senior officers. However, mere-
ly carrying out such an order would be a ministerial act, and con-
sequently, would not constitute making or participating in a gov-
ernmental decision. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d)(1).

Clayton P. Roche
Page four
June 30, 1977


     You also have asked whether other California Highway
Patrol employees may purchase gasoline for their state cars
from the Arco station in question. Section 87100 provides that
a public official shall not use his official position to influ-
ence any governmental decision which would foreseeably and ma-
terially affect his financial interest. Thus, the employee with
the ownership interest in the Arco station would be prohibited
from using his authority or rank to persuade or encourage other
employees to frequent his gas station. Any action on the employ-
ee's part which would reasonably lead his fellow employees to
fear reprisals if they did not purchase gasoline from his sta-
tion similarly would be prohibited. However, nothing in the
Act prohibits an employee of the Highway Patrol from independ-
ly deciding to purchase gasoline from the Arco station in ques-
tion so long as that employee does not have a financial interest
in the station.

     Of course, nothing in this letter is meant to comment
on the power of the Highway Patrol to prohibit certain practices
by its employees apart from the provisions of the Political Re-
form Act.

     If you have any further questions, please do not hesi-
tate to call me or Ted Prim, an attorney with the Commission's
Legal Division.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

sections.conclusion: The employee generally would be prohibited from making or participating in a decision to purchase gasoline at the Arco station in which he has a financial interest, except where a Highway Patrol directive or senior officer orders all employees to purchase from a specific station or the cheapest station, in which case the employee's compliance would be a ministerial act. Other employees may independently purchase gasoline from the Arco station as long as they do not have a financial interest in it, but the owner-employee cannot use his authority to influence or pressure other employees to purchase from his station.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a California Highway Patrol employee who owns a part interest in an Arco gas station can purchase gasoline for state vehicles from that station, and whether other employees can purchase from that station.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that the employee with an ownership interest is generally prohibited from purchasing gasoline from his own station under Government Code Section 87100, as it constitutes making a governmental decision affecting his financial interest, except where a directive makes it a ministerial act. Other employees may purchase from the station independently but the owner cannot use his position to influence them.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter responding to Attorney General Opinion Request. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Document quality slightly degraded with OCR artifacts but content is fully readable and extractable. Letter cites specific Government Code sections (87100, 87103) and FPPC regulations (18700, 18702).

You also have asked whether other California Highway 
Patrol employees may purchase gasoline for their state cars 
from the Arco station in question. 
Section 87100 provides that 
a public official shall not use his official position to influ-
ence any governmental decision which would foreseeably and ma-
terially affect his financial interest. 
Thus, the employee with 
the ownership interest in the Arco station would be prohibited 
from using his authority or rank to persuade or encourage other 
employees to frequent his gas station. 
Any action on the employ-
ee's part which would reasonably lead his fellow employees to 
fear reprisals if they did not purchase gasoline from his sta-
tion similarly would be prohibited.

ly deciding to purchase gasoline from the Arco station in ques-
tion so long as that employee does not have a financial interest 
in the station. 
Of course, nothing in this letter is meant to comment 
on the power of the Highway Patrol to prohibit certain practices 
by its employees apart from the provisions of the Political Re-
form Act.

CONCLUSION: The employee generally would be prohibited from making or participating in a decision to purchase gasoline at the Arco station in which he has a financial interest, except where a Highway Patrol directive or senior officer orders all employees to purchase from a specific station or the cheapest station, in which case the employee's compliance would be a ministerial act. Other employees may independently purchase gasoline from the Arco station as long as they do not have a financial interest in it, but the owner-employee cannot use his authority to influence or pressure other employees to purchase from his station.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises Deputy Attorney General Clayton Roche that a California Highway Patrol employee with a part interest in an Arco gas station is generally prohibited from purchasing gasoline for state vehicles from that station under the Political Reform Act's conflict of interest provisions, unless complying with a directive that makes the purchase a ministerial act.
